abstraction-with-base-interfaces.label: Abstraction With Base Interfaces
abstraction-with-base-interfaces.summary: Use CatalogItem to describe the shared `label()` interface, then let Book and Movie fulfill that interface so helpers can rely on the shared method shape.

sketch1.title: Fulfill the interface in two subclasses

sketch2.title: Use the interface in a helper

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Define the base interface gently
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: In `models/catalog_item.py`, make `label()` describe the expected interface by raising `NotImplementedError("Subclasses must define label().")`. Keep the provided `Book` subclass and use `main.py` to print the label for `Book("The Wild Robot")`.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: The base class keeps `title`, but the subclass supplies the real label.

help.concept: A base interface can describe expected behavior without giving the final answer for every subclass.
help.hint_2: Use `raise NotImplementedError(...)` in the base `label()` method.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.starterCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Wild Robot")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Wild Robot")
print(book.label())

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Wild Robot")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Wild Robot")
print(book.label())

1.message: CatalogItem should still accept a title.

2.message: Store title on the base object.

3.message: Book.label() should return the book label.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Add Movie to the same interface
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: Keep the base interface and add another subclass. In `models/movie.py`, make `Movie.label()` return `Movie: Moana`. Use the provided `main.py` to print both `Book("Wonder")` and `Movie("Moana")` labels.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: Movie should fulfill the same `label()` contract as Book.

help.concept: Two subclasses can satisfy the same interface with different results.
help.hint_2: Return `Movie: ` followed by the stored title.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Wonder")
movie = Movie("Moana")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Wonder")
movie = Movie("Moana")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Wonder")
movie = Movie("Moana")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Wonder")
movie = Movie("Moana")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

0.message: Book.label() should still return the book label.

1.message: Movie.label() should return the movie label.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Use a helper that trusts the interface
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: Keep the provided classes and helper shape. In `services/catalog_view.py`, complete `show_labels(items)` so it loops through the list and prints `item.label()` for `Book("Frindle")` and `Movie("Luca")`.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: The helper should depend only on the shared `label()` interface.

help.concept: Helpers can stay simple when each object supports the same method.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Frindle"), Movie("Luca")]
show_labels(items)

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Frindle"), Movie("Luca")]
show_labels(items)

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Frindle"), Movie("Luca")]
show_labels(items)

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Frindle"), Movie("Luca")]
show_labels(items)

0.message: Book.label() should return the expected label.

1.message: Movie.label() should return the expected label.

sc-interface-purpose.title: Why define the interface in CatalogItem?
sc-interface-purpose.prompt: Why is a base interface useful in this module?
sc-interface-purpose.hint: Think about what later code can rely on.

help.concept: A base interface tells the rest of the program which method every subclass should provide.
help.hint_1: The answer is about a shared contract.
help.hint_2: It is not about forcing every subclass to return identical text.

options.a: It tells later code that every catalog item should provide `label()`.
options.b: It prevents subclasses from changing behavior.
options.c: It removes the need for subclasses entirely.
options.d: It makes `Book` and `Movie` the same class.

fb-interface-method-name.title: Complete the shared interface method
fb-interface-method-name.prompt: Fill in the expected method name for the interface.
fb-interface-method-name.hint: Use the same method name from this module's helper examples.

help.concept: The base interface and the subclasses agree on one shared method name.

dr-interface-build-order.title: Order the interface steps
dr-interface-build-order.hint: Describe the contract first, then fulfill it, then use it.

help.concept: The interface comes before the subclasses and helpers that depend on it.
help.hint_1: The base class describes the expected method first.
help.hint_2: The helper uses the finished interface last.

tokens.t1: Write `label()` in CatalogItem as the expected interface

sc-interface-helper-focus.title: Choose what the helper should care about
sc-interface-helper-focus.prompt: When `show_labels(items)` loops through mixed objects, what should it focus on most?
sc-interface-helper-focus.hint: Pick the shared contract, not the exact subclass.

help.concept: Good interface-based code depends on shared behavior instead of subclass-specific branches.
help.hint_1: The helper should not care whether the object is a Book or Movie.
help.hint_2: It only needs the shared method to exist.

options.a: The order the subclasses were written in the file
options.b: The exact subclass name for every object
options.d: How many times the constructor was called

sketch-0.title: A base class can describe the expected method

sketch-1.title: Subclasses fulfill the interface
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A subclass completes the contract by writing its own method.

```python
class Book(CatalogItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Book: {self.title}"
```

`Book` now matches the interface described by the base class.

sketch-2.title: Code can rely on the interface
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
A helper function only needs the shared interface.

```python
def show_labels(items):
    for item in items:
        print(item.label())
```

The helper does not care whether each item is a `Book` or a `Movie`, only that each one has `label()`.
